onboarding.done: You're all set! Feeling is placed on the Russell circumplex model — two axes: • Valence: how pleasant (+ ) or unpleasant (−) • Arousal: how energised (+) or calm (−) Your first check-in will arrive in {min}–{max} hours.
settings.title: Settings
settings.weekly_status: Weekly summary is {status}. Sent on {day} at 09:00.
stats.not_enough_data: Not enough data yet — you need at least {min} check-ins.
help.text: Feelinq — your mood journal in Telegram. How it works: 1. You'll get a check-in a few times a day 2. Pick the emotions that fit 3. Over time, see your patterns in /stats Commands: /settings — reminders, emotions, timezone /stats — your mood charts /theory — the science behind Feelinq /feedback — send us a message
theory.text: Why this approach? A single "rate your mood 1–10" scale misses a lot. Think about it: Astonished and Calm are both positive emotions — but they feel completely different. One is high-energy, the other is peaceful. A single number can't tell them apart. So alongside pleasantness we need a second scale — intensity. Researchers who study emotions discovered that this isn't a coincidence: our feelings map onto two independent neurophysiological systems in the brain [1] [2]. This is the basis of the Russell circumplex model [3] — a 2D map of emotions: • Valence — how pleasant or unpleasant • Arousal — how energised or calm During a check-in you can pick several emotions at once — because our feelings are usually a mix of many things. Feelinq averages them into a single point on the map. Over time, /stats reveals where you tend to land, how your energy shifts through the day, and which emotions come up most.
feedback.sent: Thanks! Your feedback has been sent.
feedback.missing: Please add your message: /feedback your text here
admin.stats: Admin stats Total users: {total_users} Active (7d): {active_7d} Total entries: {total_entries} Platforms: {platforms}
